title: Heatwave Health Crisis: How Rising Temperatures Are Triggering Kidney and UTI Cases - The Indian Practitioner
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
As heatwaves intensify across India, soaring temperatures are doing more than causing discomfort and dehydration. They are increasingly contributing to kidney problems and urinary tract infections (UTIs). Prolonged exposure to heat leads to significant fluid loss, which places stress on the kidneys and creates favourable conditions for bacterial growth in the urinary tract. Therefore, understanding the health risks associated with extreme heat and adopting preventive measures has become essential.
Why Heat Increases the Risk of Kidney Problems
During intense heat, the body loses large amounts of fluids through sweating. If individuals fail to replenish these fluids adequately, dehydration occurs. Consequently, dehydration reduces blood flow to the kidneys and impairs their ability to function efficiently.
As a result, individuals may develop conditions such as kidney stones, acute kidney injury, or worsening of pre-existing kidney disease. In many cases, kidney-related issues present with symptoms including lower back pain, reduced urine output, swelling in the hands or feet, fatigue, and nausea. Additionally, severe dehydration can lead to dizziness and confusion, which may disrupt daily activities.
If left untreated, kidney complications may escalate into serious conditions such as kidney failure, electrolyte imbalance, or long-term kidney damage that may require dialysis.
How Heatwaves Contribute to Urinary Tract Infections
At the same time, rising temperatures also increase the likelihood of urinary tract infections. Dehydration leads to reduced urination, which allows bacteria to remain and multiply in the urinary tract. Moreover, infrequent urination, poor hygiene, and prolonged exposure to heat further raise the risk of infection.
Common symptoms of UTIs include a burning sensation during urination, frequent urges to urinate, cloudy or foul-smelling urine, lower abdominal discomfort, and fever. If individuals ignore these warning signs, the infection can spread to the kidneys and cause pyelonephritis, a severe and potentially life-threatening condition.
Who Is Most Vulnerable?
Certain groups face a higher risk of heat-related kidney issues and UTIs. For instance, urban populations exposed to high temperatures, outdoor workers who spend long hours in the sun, and older adults with reduced physiological resilience are particularly vulnerable. Therefore, these groups must remain especially cautious during extreme heat conditions.
Prevention: Protecting Kidney and Urinary Health in Summer
Fortunately, several preventive steps can help reduce the risk of heat-related kidney problems and UTIs. First and foremost, individuals should prioritize adequate hydration by drinking at least two to three litres of water daily. Proper hydration helps maintain kidney function and flushes bacteria from the urinary tract.
In addition, limiting excessive caffeine and alcohol intake is important, as these substances can further dehydrate the body. Maintaining good personal hygiene and urinating regularly also play crucial roles in preventing UTIs. Furthermore, wearing breathable and comfortable clothing helps reduce bacterial growth in warm and moist environments.
To protect kidney health, individuals should also moderate salt intake, avoid prolonged sun exposure, and rest during peak heat hours. Taking these precautions can significantly reduce the strain that extreme temperatures place on the body.
The Last Word
Extreme heat poses a serious yet often overlooked threat to kidney and urinary health. However, individuals can minimize these risks by staying hydrated, maintaining hygiene, and recognizing early symptoms of infection or kidney distress. Most importantly, seeking timely medical attention for symptoms of UTIs or kidney problems allows doctors to initiate appropriate treatment, such as antibiotics for UTIs or hydration therapy for kidney-related complications.
This summer, stay vigilant, follow preventive measures, and protect your kidney and urinary health from the impact of rising temperatures.

title: At the gates of an atypical heatwave for the time of year - en.ara.cat

full_text:
BarcelonaThe absolute calm of the Easter holidays has continued this Tuesday, with general sunshine and a warm midday atmosphere that has left highs of up to 27 ºC in Ponent. Temperatures that are high for the season, which are still expected to peak in the coming days, when the first 30 ºC of the year could be reached inland. Values clearly above the usual for this time of year and more typical of late spring or early summer. All of this is the result of a low-pressure system located between the southwestern Iberian Peninsula and the Canary Islands, which, with its counterclockwise rotation, will drive warm winds from North Africa towards home, as well as an incursion of Saharan dust in suspension.
Wednesday: hotter
Temperatures will rise more this Wednesday, both minimum and maximum. Very moderate cold or cool in the early hours, and warm atmosphere at midday, with many highs from 24 ºC to 28 ºC, although locally 30 ºC will be reached in the south and in Poniente. Thermometers more restrained on the coast due to the regulating effect of a still quite cold sea. The contrast between the cool sea water and the environmental mildness will favor the appearance of some fogs or low clouds in coastal stretches.
Beyond these nuances on the coast, the sun will dominate in the whole country, with only some bands of high, thin clouds. Suspended dust will increase, which will favor the increase of haze.
Heat peak in the coming days
Between Thursday and Saturday, the heat peak will arrive, with temperatures that will still soar higher. Most values will range between 25 ºC and 30 ºC in the pre-coastal and inland areas, which will likely lead to the first 30 ºC of this year being reached in our region. If this happens, this value will be reached almost a month earlier than last year.
A very warm atmosphere for the dates, which will place thermometers up to ten degrees above the usual average for this time of year in some areas, especially in the Pyrenees. The irruption of African dust in suspension will reach its peak at the end of the week. It will be then when the weather will begin to change.
With current maps, between Sunday and Monday, a cold air mass would arrive, causing precipitation everywhere and a clear cooling. We will have to keep confirming this, as there are still days until it arrives.
